expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of virus structure—the fundamental architectural components that define viral particles including capsid symmetry, envelope composition, and nucleic acid diversity—detailing how structural features determine host range, transmission mechanisms, and vulnerability to environmental conditions, with emphasis on the relationship between viral architecture and replication strategies.

core_concept.principle: Viruses are acellular infectious particles composed of genetic material (DNA or RNA) enclosed within a protein capsid, with some possessing a lipid envelope derived from host membranes; structural organization determines host specificity, transmission routes, and environmental stability.
core_concept.explanation: Viruses represent the simplest biological entities, lacking cellular organization and metabolic machinery, yet exhibiting remarkable structural diversity adapted to specific hosts and transmission strategies. The viral particle (virion) consists of: (1) Nucleic acid genome: either DNA or RNA, single- or double-stranded, linear or circular, segmented or non-segmented, ranging from ~3 kb (parvoviruses) to ~2.5 Mb (pandoraviruses); (2) Capsid: protein shell composed of repeating subunits (capsomeres) arranged in icosahedral, helical, or complex symmetry, protecting the genome and facilitating host cell attachment; (3) Envelope (optional): lipid bilayer derived from host membranes (plasma membrane, nuclear envelope, Golgi) studded with viral glycoproteins that mediate host cell entry. Non-enveloped (naked) viruses are generally more stable in the environment; enveloped viruses are more sensitive to desiccation, detergents, and disinfectants but often achieve more efficient cell entry. Surface proteins (spikes, fibers) determine host range by binding specific cellular receptors. Understanding viral structure is essential for developing antiviral strategies, vaccines, and diagnostic tools, as structural features represent primary targets for intervention.

image.alt: Diagram comparing enveloped and non-enveloped virus structures with labeled components
image.caption: Virus structure: (A) Non-enveloped icosahedral virus: capsid (capsomeres) + nucleic acid. (B) Enveloped helical virus: nucleocapsid (RNA + capsid proteins) + lipid envelope + glycoprotein spikes. Structural features determine host range, stability, and entry mechanisms.

sections.introduction: Viruses challenge traditional definitions of life: they possess genetic material and evolve through natural selection, yet lack cellular organization, metabolism, and independent reproduction. First recognized in the late 19th century as 'filterable agents' causing tobacco mosaic disease and foot-and-mouth disease, viruses were visualized only after the invention of the electron microscope in the 1930s. Despite their simplicity, viruses exhibit extraordinary structural diversity adapted to infect organisms across all domains of life—bacteria (bacteriophages), archaea, plants, animals, and even other viruses (virophages). Viral architecture reflects evolutionary optimization: minimal genetic information encodes structures that efficiently protect the genome, recognize host cells, deliver genetic material, and assemble new particles. The study of virus structure (virion morphology) provides insights into host-pathogen interactions, mechanisms of infection, and targets for antiviral therapies. This section explores the fundamental components of viral particles—nucleic acids, capsids, and envelopes—and how structural variations influence viral ecology, transmission, and control.

introduction_image.alt: Historical timeline of virus discovery from filterable agents to cryo-EM structures
introduction_image.caption: Virus discovery: 1892 (Ivanovsky, tobacco mosaic virus) → 1935 (Stanley, TMV crystallization) → 1939 (first EM images) → 1950s (capsid symmetry elucidated) → 2010s (cryo-EM atomic structures). Each advance revealed greater structural complexity.

key_concept.definition: A virus is an acellular infectious particle consisting of nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) enclosed within a protein capsid, with some viruses possessing a host-derived lipid envelope studded with viral glycoproteins.
key_concept.context: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites that require host cellular machinery for replication. Classification follows the Baltimore system based on genome type and replication strategy (7 groups). Structural features (capsid symmetry, envelope presence, surface proteins) determine host range, tissue tropism, environmental stability, and susceptibility to disinfectants. Viral particles range from ~20 nm (parvoviruses) to ~1.5 µm (pandoraviruses), with most pathogenic human viruses between 20-300 nm.

image.alt: Size comparison of viruses, bacteria, and eukaryotic cells with scale bar
image.caption: Virus sizes: Parvovirus (20 nm), poliovirus (30 nm), influenza (100 nm), smallpox (300 nm), pandoravirus (1.5 µm). Viruses are generally 10-100× smaller than bacteria, enabling filtration and aerosol transmission.

sections.key_concept: Viral structure comprises three fundamental components—nucleic acid genome, protein capsid, and optional envelope—each contributing to host recognition, genome protection, and particle assembly.

classifications.type: Viral Nucleic Acids: Genome Diversity and Organization
classifications.value: Best for understanding how genome type influences replication strategy, mutation rate, and evolutionary potential
characteristics: DNA viruses: Double-stranded (dsDNA: herpesviruses, poxviruses) or single-stranded (ssDNA: parvoviruses); generally lower mutation rates due to host DNA polymerase proofreading

RNA viruses: Double-stranded (dsRNA: reoviruses) or single-stranded (ssRNA); ssRNA classified as positive-sense (+ssRNA: coronaviruses, directly translatable) or negative-sense (-ssRNA: influenza, requires viral polymerase); high mutation rates due to error-prone RNA polymerases
Genome segmentation: Some viruses have segmented genomes (influenza: 8 RNA segments) enabling reassortment and rapid evolution
Genome size range: ~3 kb (parvoviruses) to ~2.5 Mb (pandoraviruses); most human pathogens 5-300 kb
Terminal structures: Linear genomes may have covalently attached proteins (adenovirus), hairpin loops (parvovirus), or poly-A tails (+ssRNA viruses)
classifications.behavior: Genome type fundamentally shapes viral biology: dsDNA viruses often replicate in the nucleus using host machinery, enabling larger genomes and complex regulation; RNA viruses replicate in cytoplasm with viral polymerases, favoring small genomes and high mutation rates that facilitate rapid adaptation (antigenic drift). Segmented genomes enable reassortment when two strains co-infect a cell, generating novel combinations (antigenic shift in influenza). Positive-sense RNA genomes function directly as mRNA, enabling immediate protein synthesis upon entry; negative-sense genomes require packaged viral polymerase to transcribe mRNA first. Genome organization (overlapping reading frames, subgenomic RNAs) maximizes coding capacity within size constraints imposed by capsid assembly.

examples.process: Influenza Virus Segmented Genome and Antigenic Shift
examples.explanation: Influenza A virus has 8 negative-sense ssRNA segments encoding 11+ proteins via alternative splicing and overlapping reading frames. Segments: PB2, PB1, PA (polymerase), HA (hemagglutinin), NP (nucleoprotein), NA (neuraminidase), M1/M2 (matrix), NS1/NS2 (nonstructural). Segmentation enables reassortment: when two strains co-infect a cell, segments mix, producing progeny with novel combinations. Antigenic shift occurs when human and avian influenza strains reassort, generating HA/NA combinations to which humans lack immunity (e.g., 1957 H2N2, 1968 H3N2, 2009 H1N1 pandemics). Antigenic drift results from point mutations in HA/NA, requiring annual vaccine updates. Segmentation thus balances evolutionary flexibility with functional constraints.

image.alt: Influenza virus genome segments and reassortment mechanism
image.caption: Influenza genome: 8 -ssRNA segments encoding polymerase, surface proteins (HA, NA), internal proteins. Reassortment during co-infection generates novel strains (antigenic shift); point mutations cause antigenic drift.

examples.explanation: +ssRNA viruses (coronaviruses, poliovirus): Genome functions directly as mRNA; upon entry, host ribosomes translate viral polyprotein, which is cleaved into functional proteins including RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp). RdRp then synthesizes negative-sense template for genomic RNA production. Advantage: immediate protein synthesis; disadvantage: genome vulnerable to host nucleases. -ssRNA viruses (influenza, rabies): Genome is complementary to mRNA; virion must package RdRp to transcribe mRNA upon entry. Advantage: genome protected as template; disadvantage: delayed protein synthesis. dsRNA viruses (rotavirus): Genome segments are transcribed within intact capsid by packaged polymerase, protecting dsRNA from host antiviral sensors (which detect cytoplasmic dsRNA as pathogen-associated molecular pattern).

image.alt: Replication strategies of +ssRNA, -ssRNA, and dsRNA viruses
image.caption: +ssRNA: Genome = mRNA → immediate translation. -ssRNA: Requires packaged RdRp to make mRNA first. dsRNA: Transcription within capsid avoids host dsRNA sensors. Genome sense determines entry-to-replication timeline.

classifications.type: Capsid Architecture: Symmetry and Assembly Principles
classifications.value: Best for understanding how protein subunits self-assemble into protective shells with defined geometry
characteristics: Icosahedral symmetry: 20 triangular faces, 12 vertices; most efficient way to enclose space with identical subunits; T-number (triangulation number) defines subunit arrangement (T=1, 3, 7, 13, etc.)
Helical symmetry: Capsid proteins bind nucleic acid in spiral arrangement; length proportional to genome size; diameter fixed by protein-protein interactions
Complex symmetry: Bacteriophages with icosahedral heads + helical tails + tail fibers; poxviruses with brick-shaped morphology and lateral bodies
Capsomeres: Morphological subunits visible by EM; may be pentamers (at vertices) or hexamers (on faces) in icosahedral viruses
Quasi-equivalence: Identical capsid proteins adopt slightly different conformations to form curved surfaces (Caspar-Klug theory)
classifications.behavior: Capsid assembly follows principles of symmetry and self-organization: identical or quasi-identical protein subunits interact through non-covalent bonds (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic forces) to form stable shells. Icosahedral symmetry minimizes genetic information required (one or few capsid protein genes) while maximizing internal volume. T-number reflects how many quasi-equivalent conformations a single protein adopts; higher T-numbers accommodate larger genomes. Helical capsids assemble co-transcriptionally: capsid proteins bind nascent RNA, forming nucleocapsid as genome is replicated. Complex viruses (bacteriophages) use assembly pathways with scaffolding proteins that are discarded after capsid formation. Capsid stability balances genome protection with controlled disassembly during cell entry.

examples.explanation: Icosahedral capsids follow Caspar-Klug quasi-equivalence theory: identical protein subunits adopt slightly different conformations to form a closed shell with icosahedral symmetry. Triangulation number (T) = h² + hk + k², where h and k are steps on a hexagonal lattice. T=1: 60 subunits (12 pentamers); T=3: 180 subunits (12 pentamers + 20 hexamers); T=7: 420 subunits, etc. Example: Poliovirus (T=3) has 60 asymmetric units, each containing 3 quasi-equivalent copies of each capsid protein (VP1, VP2, VP3), totaling 180 subunits. Assembly: Proteins form pentamers → 12 pentamers assemble into procapsid → RNA packaged → conformational change matures capsid. T-number evolution balances genome size constraints with assembly efficiency.

image.alt: Icosahedral capsid structure showing T=3 symmetry with pentamers and hexamers
image.caption: Icosahedral symmetry: 20 faces, 12 vertices. T=3 capsid: 12 pentamers (vertices) + 20 hexamers (faces) = 180 subunits. Quasi-equivalent conformations enable curved surface from identical proteins.

examples.explanation: Bacteriophage T4 exemplifies complex viral architecture: icosahedral head (capsid) contains dsDNA genome; helical tail sheath surrounds inner tube; baseplate with tail fibers mediates host recognition. Assembly is modular and sequential: (1) Head: Scaffolding proteins guide capsid protein assembly → DNA packaged by terminase motor (ATP-driven) → scaffolding discarded; (2) Tail: Baseplate assembles first → tail tube/sheath polymerize → tail fibers attach; (3) Maturation: Head and tail join via connector protein. Tail fibers recognize E. coli surface receptors (OmpC, LPS); baseplate conformational change triggers sheath contraction, driving inner tube through cell envelope to inject DNA. This complex structure enables precise host targeting and efficient genome delivery.

image.alt: Bacteriophage T4 structure showing head, tail, baseplate, and tail fibers
image.caption: Phage T4: Icosahedral head (DNA), helical tail sheath, baseplate, tail fibers. Modular assembly: head + tail + fibers → mature virion. Tail fibers recognize host; sheath contraction injects DNA.

classifications.type: Viral Envelopes: Host-Derived Membranes and Glycoprotein Spikes
classifications.value: Best for understanding how viruses acquire host membranes and use glycoproteins for cell entry and immune evasion
characteristics: Envelope origin: Derived from host membranes—plasma membrane (HIV, influenza), nuclear envelope (herpesviruses), Golgi/ER (coronaviruses)
Glycoprotein spikes: Viral transmembrane proteins (hemagglutinin, gp120, S protein) mediate receptor binding and membrane fusion
Matrix proteins: Underlie envelope, linking glycoproteins to nucleocapsid (M protein in influenza, matrix in HIV)
Lipid composition: Reflects host membrane of origin; enriched in cholesterol and sphingolipids in lipid rafts
Envelope acquisition: Budding through host membrane incorporates viral glycoproteins while acquiring lipid bilayer
classifications.behavior: Enveloped viruses acquire their lipid bilayer by budding through host membranes, a process that incorporates viral glycoproteins while excluding most host proteins. Glycoproteins are synthesized in ER, glycosylated in Golgi, and transported to budding site. Budding is mediated by interactions between matrix proteins (or capsid proteins in some viruses) and cytoplasmic tails of glycoproteins, coupled to host ESCRT (endosomal sorting complex required for transport) machinery that pinches off the virion. Envelopes confer advantages: glycoproteins enable efficient receptor binding and membrane fusion; envelope shields capsid from antibodies; budding avoids cell lysis, enabling persistent infection. Disadvantages: envelopes are sensitive to desiccation, detergents, and disinfectants (enveloped viruses are easier to inactivate than naked viruses). Envelope glycoproteins are primary targets for neutralizing antibodies, driving antigenic variation as an immune evasion strategy.

examples.process: Influenza Hemagglutinin: Receptor Binding and Membrane Fusion
examples.explanation: Influenza hemagglutinin (HA) is a trimeric class I fusion glycoprotein that mediates both receptor binding and membrane fusion. Structure: HA0 precursor cleaved by host proteases into HA1 (receptor binding) and HA2 (fusion). HA1 binds sialic acid receptors on respiratory epithelial cells; specificity for α2,6-linked (human) vs. α2,3-linked (avian) sialic acids determines host range. Upon endocytosis, low pH in endosome triggers HA2 conformational change: fusion peptide inserts into endosomal membrane, HA2 folds back on itself (hairpin formation), pulling viral and endosomal membranes together to drive fusion. This pH-dependent mechanism ensures fusion occurs only after endocytosis, preventing premature activation. Antigenic sites on HA1 are targets for neutralizing antibodies; mutations in these sites (antigenic drift) enable immune escape.

image.alt: Hemagglutinin conformational changes during receptor binding and membrane fusion
image.caption: Hemagglutinin: HA1 binds sialic acid receptor; low pH triggers HA2 refolding → fusion peptide insertion → membrane merger. pH-dependence ensures fusion occurs post-endocytosis. Antigenic sites on HA1 drive immune escape evolution.

principles.principle: Viral Structural Simplicity Reflects Evolutionary Optimization for Host Exploitation
principles.statement: Viruses minimize genetic information by encoding multifunctional proteins and exploiting host machinery, with structural components serving multiple roles in attachment, entry, assembly, and immune evasion.
principles.application: Viral genomes are compact: overlapping reading frames, alternative splicing, and polyprotein processing maximize coding capacity. Capsid proteins often have dual functions: structural assembly and receptor binding (e.g., adenovirus fiber protein). Envelope glycoproteins mediate both attachment and fusion. Some viruses incorporate host proteins into virions (e.g., HIV incorporates host ICAM-1 to enhance infectivity). This economy of design enables rapid replication and high mutation rates, facilitating adaptation to host defenses and environmental changes. However, structural minimalism creates vulnerabilities: dependence on host factors limits host range; small genomes constrain evolutionary options; simple structures present conserved targets for antivirals.
principles.example: HIV envelope glycoprotein complexity: gp160 precursor cleaved into gp120 (receptor binding) and gp41 (fusion). gp120 binds CD4 receptor, then undergoes conformational change to bind CCR5/CXCR4 co-receptor, triggering gp41-mediated fusion. This multi-step process ensures specificity for target cells (CD4⁺ T cells, macrophages). However, gp120 is heavily glycosylated ('glycan shield') to mask epitopes from antibodies, and hypervariable loops enable antigenic variation. The same protein thus balances receptor specificity, fusion activation, and immune evasion—demonstrating structural multifunctionality.
principles.analogy: Viral structure is like a Swiss Army knife: minimal components perform multiple functions. Capsid proteins are both building blocks and keys (receptor binding); envelope glycoproteins are both locks (receptor specificity) and levers (membrane fusion). This multifunctionality enables efficiency but creates trade-offs: optimizing one function may compromise another, driving evolutionary balancing acts.

image.alt: Examples of viral proteins with multiple structural and functional roles
image.caption: Multifunctional viral proteins: Capsid proteins (assembly + receptor binding), envelope glycoproteins (attachment + fusion), polymerases (replication + capping). Structural economy enables compact genomes but creates evolutionary constraints.

principles.principle: Capsid Symmetry and Assembly Mechanisms Constrain Genome Size and Evolution
principles.statement: The geometric principles governing capsid assembly impose physical limits on genome packaging, influencing viral evolution and host adaptation strategies.
principles.application: Icosahedral capsids follow Caspar-Klug quasi-equivalence: genome size correlates with capsid volume, which scales with T-number. Small T-numbers (T=1-3) accommodate genomes <10 kb (parvoviruses, picornaviruses); larger T-numbers enable bigger genomes but require more complex assembly pathways. Helical capsids scale linearly with genome length but have fixed diameter, limiting genome thickness. Complex viruses (bacteriophages) overcome size constraints with specialized packaging motors that compress DNA to near-crystalline density (~500 mg/mL). These physical constraints shape viral evolution: RNA viruses favor small genomes with high mutation rates for rapid adaptation; dsDNA viruses evolve larger genomes with proofreading polymerases for stability. Genome expansion often requires acquisition of host-derived genes (e.g., immune modulators in poxviruses), blurring boundaries between viral and cellular evolution.
principles.example: Adeno-associated virus (AAV) capsid engineering: Wild-type AAV (T=1, ~25 nm diameter) packages ~4.7 kb ssDNA. Gene therapy applications require larger payloads; engineering approaches include: (1) Dual-vector systems (split genome reassembles in cell); (2) Capsid mutants with expanded volume (directed evolution); (3) Hybrid capsids incorporating proteins from larger parvoviruses. These strategies illustrate how physical constraints on capsid assembly drive biotechnological innovation, while highlighting evolutionary trade-offs between genome size and assembly efficiency.
principles.analogy: Capsid assembly is like packing a suitcase: icosahedral symmetry is a rigid, geometrically efficient design that fits specific volumes (genome sizes); helical symmetry is a flexible tube that scales with content length. Trying to pack too much (large genome) into a small case (low T-number) causes assembly failure; evolving a bigger case (high T-number) requires more complex instructions (additional assembly factors). Physical constraints shape what can evolve.

image.alt: Relationship between capsid symmetry, T-number, and maximum genome size
image.caption: Capsid constraints: Icosahedral volume ∝ T^(3/2); helical length ∝ genome size. Physical limits on packaging density (~500 mg/mL for dsDNA) constrain genome expansion. Evolution balances genome size with assembly efficiency.

principles.principle: Envelope Presence Determines Environmental Stability and Transmission Routes
principles.statement: The presence or absence of a lipid envelope fundamentally influences viral susceptibility to environmental stresses, disinfectants, and transmission mechanisms.
principles.application: Non-enveloped (naked) viruses (poliovirus, norovirus, adenovirus) have protein capsids resistant to desiccation, detergents, and acidic pH, enabling fecal-oral transmission and environmental persistence (days to weeks on surfaces). Enveloped viruses (influenza, HIV, SARS-CoV-2) have lipid bilayers sensitive to desiccation, detergents, alcohols, and acidic pH, limiting environmental survival (hours) but enabling efficient cell entry via membrane fusion. Envelope sensitivity explains disinfection strategies: alcohol-based sanitizers inactivate enveloped viruses but require higher concentrations or longer contact times for naked viruses. Transmission routes correlate with envelope status: enveloped viruses favor respiratory droplets, blood, or sexual contact (protected environments); naked viruses tolerate fecal-oral, fomite, or waterborne transmission. Understanding envelope properties informs public health interventions and infection control protocols.
principles.example: Norovirus vs. influenza stability: Norovirus (naked, icosahedral) survives weeks on surfaces, resists alcohol sanitizers (requires bleach), and transmits via fecal-oral route due to environmental hardiness. Influenza (enveloped, helical) survives hours on surfaces, inactivated by alcohol sanitizers, and transmits via respiratory droplets requiring close contact. These differences dictate control measures: norovirus outbreaks require rigorous surface disinfection and handwashing with soap; influenza control emphasizes respiratory hygiene and alcohol-based sanitizers. Envelope status thus directly shapes epidemiology and intervention strategies.
principles.analogy: Viral envelopes are like raincoats: enveloped viruses wear a lipid 'raincoat' that enables efficient entry (fusion) but washes off easily (sensitive to detergents, desiccation); naked viruses go 'bare' with a tough protein 'skin' that withstands harsh conditions but requires more forceful entry mechanisms. The choice of 'attire' determines where and how the virus can travel and infect.

image.alt: Comparison of enveloped vs. non-enveloped virus stability and transmission routes
image.caption: Envelope impact: Naked viruses (poliovirus) resist desiccation, detergents, acid → fecal-oral transmission, environmental persistence. Enveloped viruses (influenza) sensitive to desiccation, detergents → respiratory/blood transmission, limited environmental survival.

components.definition: Viral particles integrate nucleic acid genome, protein capsid, and optional envelope into functional units optimized for host cell recognition, genome delivery, and particle assembly.
components.interpretation: Viral fitness depends on coordinated function of structural components: genome encodes replication instructions; capsid protects genome and mediates initial host interactions; envelope (if present) facilitates entry and immune evasion. Assembly pathways ensure proper stoichiometry and geometry: capsid proteins self-assemble around genome or scaffolding; envelope glycoproteins traffic to budding sites; matrix proteins coordinate nucleocapsid-envelope interactions. Structural features represent primary targets for antiviral strategies: capsid assembly inhibitors, entry blockers targeting glycoproteins, and envelope-disrupting disinfectants. Understanding component interactions enables rational design of vaccines (virus-like particles mimicking capsid structure) and therapeutics.

image.alt: Integrated diagram of viral particle showing genome, capsid, envelope, and surface proteins
image.caption: Virion integration: Genome (replication instructions) + capsid (protection/assembly) + envelope (entry/immune evasion) + surface proteins (receptor binding). Coordinated function enables infection cycle.

categories.description: Viral capsid proteins exhibit conserved structural motifs that enable self-assembly into symmetric shells through non-covalent interactions
categories.detailed_mechanism: Capsid proteins share common folds despite low sequence similarity: jelly-roll β-barrel (most icosahedral viruses), HK97 fold (bacteriophages, herpesviruses), or helical bundle (tobacco mosaic virus). Assembly is driven by: (1) Hydrophobic interactions between subunit interfaces; (2) Electrostatic complementarity (positive capsid interiors bind negative nucleic acids); (3) Conformational flexibility enabling quasi-equivalent interactions. Assembly pathways vary: some viruses assemble empty procapsids then package genome (bacteriophages, herpesviruses); others co-assemble capsid and genome (picornaviruses, TMV). Scaffolding proteins often guide assembly but are discarded in mature virions. Maturation involves proteolytic cleavage or conformational changes that stabilize the capsid for environmental exposure.

examples.explanation: HIV capsid is conical, composed of ~1,500 copies of CA protein arranged in hexamers and 12 pentamers (fullerene cone). Assembly: Gag polyprotein (matrix-CA-nucleocapsid) traffics to plasma membrane → Gag multimerizes via CA-CA interactions → budding incorporates Gag-RNA complexes → viral protease cleaves Gag during/after budding → CA rearranges into mature conical capsid surrounding ribonucleoprotein complex. Uncoating: After entry, capsid disassembles in cytoplasm, releasing genome for reverse transcription. Capsid stability is critical: too stable → genome not released; too unstable → genome degraded. Host factors (CPSF6, NUP153) interact with capsid to regulate uncoating timing and nuclear import. Capsid is target for antivirals (lenacapavir stabilizes capsid, blocking uncoating).

image.alt: HIV capsid structure showing conical arrangement of CA hexamers and pentamers
image.caption: HIV capsid: Conical fullerene structure (hexamers + 12 pentamers). Assembly: Gag multimerization → budding → protease cleavage → maturation. Uncoating: Host factors regulate disassembly timing for nuclear import.

categories.description: Viral envelope glycoproteins are synthesized via host secretory pathway and incorporated into budding virions through specific targeting signals
categories.detailed_mechanism: Envelope glycoproteins follow host membrane protein trafficking: translation on ER-bound ribosomes → co-translational translocation into ER lumen → N-linked glycosylation → folding with chaperones (calnexin/calreticulin) → oligomerization (trimers for class I fusion proteins) → Golgi transport → further glycosylation → transport to plasma membrane or intracellular budding sites. Cytoplasmic tails contain motifs (YXXΦ, [DE]XXXL[LI]) that interact with host trafficking machinery (AP complexes, ESCRT) to direct glycoproteins to budding sites. During budding, interactions between glycoprotein cytoplasmic tails and matrix/capsid proteins ensure glycoprotein incorporation into virions. Glycosylation serves multiple functions: proper folding, immune evasion (glycan shield), receptor binding modulation. Defects in glycosylation or trafficking reduce infectivity, highlighting the dependence on host machinery.

examples.process: SARS-CoV-2 Spike Protein Maturation and Incorporation
examples.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 spike (S) glycoprotein is synthesized as precursor S0 → cleaved by furin at S1/S2 boundary during Golgi transit → S1 (receptor binding) and S2 (fusion) remain non-covalently associated. S trimerizes in ER, undergoes extensive N-glycosylation (~22 sites per protomer), and traffics to ER-Golgi intermediate compartment (ERGIC) where virus buds. Cytoplasmic tail contains ER retrieval signal (KxHxx motif) that may regulate incorporation efficiency. S protein incorporates into virions at ~24 trimers per particle. Glycans shield epitopes from antibodies but also modulate ACE2 binding affinity. Furin pre-cleavage primes S for efficient entry; mutations at cleavage site (e.g., D614G) enhance infectivity. Understanding S protein biogenesis informs vaccine design (stabilizing prefusion conformation) and antiviral strategies (blocking cleavage or glycosylation).

image.alt: SARS-CoV-2 spike protein biosynthesis from ER to virion incorporation
image.caption: Spike maturation: ER synthesis → furin cleavage (S1/S2) → glycosylation → trimerization → ERGIC trafficking → virion incorporation. Glycans shield epitopes; cleavage primes fusion. Biosynthesis dependencies inform therapeutic targeting.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Analyzing Viral Structure from Experimental Data

worked_examples.title: Determining Virus Morphology and Components from Microscopy and Biochemistry
worked_examples.problem: You isolate a novel virus from respiratory samples. Experimental data: (a) Electron microscopy shows ~100 nm particles with surface spikes and internal density; (b) Sucrose gradient centrifugation reveals buoyant density of 1.18 g/cm³; (c) Treatment with chloroform (lipid solvent) abolishes infectivity; (d) RNase treatment of purified virions degrades genome; (e) SDS-PAGE of purified particles shows three major proteins: 180 kDa, 28 kDa, and 12 kDa. Using structural principles, determine: (1) Likely envelope status and genome type; (2) Probable capsid symmetry; (3) Functional assignments for the three proteins; (4) Predicted environmental stability and disinfection sensitivity.

step_by_step.explanation: Interpret envelope status and genome type from biochemical data.
step_by_step.calculation: Chloroform sensitivity → lipid envelope present (chloroform disrupts lipid bilayers). RNase degrades genome in purified virions → genome is RNA (not protected by capsid alone; envelope may not fully shield genome from nucleases if capsid is compromised, but RNase sensitivity of intact virions strongly suggests RNA genome). Buoyant density 1.18 g/cm³ is typical for enveloped RNA viruses (enveloped viruses: 1.15-1.25 g/cm³; naked viruses: 1.30-1.45 g/cm³ due to higher protein:nucleic acid ratio).

step_by_step.explanation: Infer capsid symmetry from size and protein composition.
step_by_step.calculation: ~100 nm diameter with surface spikes suggests enveloped virus with icosahedral or helical nucleocapsid. Three major proteins: large (180 kDa) likely envelope glycoprotein (spikes); medium (28 kDa) likely capsid protein; small (12 kDa) likely matrix or nucleocapsid protein. Icosahedral enveloped viruses (e.g., coronaviruses, flaviviruses) often have glycoprotein spikes + capsid + matrix; helical enveloped viruses (influenza, paramyxoviruses) have glycoproteins + nucleocapsid protein + matrix. Size (~100 nm) and spike morphology favor icosahedral symmetry (helical nucleocapsids often appear pleomorphic).

step_by_step.explanation: Assign functions to observed proteins based on size and context.
step_by_step.calculation: 180 kDa protein: Large size, surface spikes → envelope glycoprotein (receptor binding/fusion); likely trimeric (60 kDa protomer × 3). 28 kDa protein: Abundant, internal → capsid/nucleocapsid protein binding RNA genome. 12 kDa protein: Small, likely peripheral → matrix protein linking nucleocapsid to envelope. This matches architecture of many enveloped RNA viruses (e.g., coronaviruses: S protein ~180 kDa, N protein ~45 kDa, M protein ~25 kDa; sizes vary by virus).

step_by_step.explanation: Predict environmental stability and disinfection sensitivity.
step_by_step.calculation: Enveloped virus → sensitive to desiccation, detergents, alcohols, acidic pH. Predicted stability: hours on surfaces at room temperature; minutes to hours in aerosols. Disinfection: alcohol-based sanitizers (60-90% ethanol/isopropanol) effective; soap/detergents disrupt envelope; bleach effective but may be overkill. Transmission likely via respiratory droplets or direct contact (not fecal-oral due to envelope sensitivity). These predictions guide infection control: emphasize respiratory hygiene, alcohol sanitizers, and surface cleaning with detergents.

worked_examples.final_answer: Virus classification: Enveloped RNA virus, likely icosahedral symmetry. Protein assignments: 180 kDa = envelope glycoprotein (spikes), 28 kDa = capsid/nucleocapsid protein, 12 kDa = matrix protein. Environmental stability: Low (hours on surfaces); disinfection: alcohol sanitizers, detergents effective. This analysis demonstrates how structural principles (envelope sensitivity, protein size-function relationships, density correlations) enable inference of viral architecture from experimental data, guiding further characterization and control strategies.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Viral structure inference integrates multiple data types: EM morphology (size, spikes), biochemical properties (chloroform sensitivity → envelope; RNase sensitivity → RNA genome), biophysical data (buoyant density), and protein composition. Envelope status determines environmental stability and disinfection strategies; protein sizes suggest functional roles based on known viral architectures. This systematic approach enables rapid characterization of novel pathogens.

sections.introduction: Viral assembly follows ordered pathways that coordinate genome packaging, capsid formation, and envelope acquisition. Understanding this flow reveals how structural components integrate into infectious particles.

process_flow.process: Genome Replication and Capsid Protein Synthesis
process_flow.description: Viral genome replication and structural protein production occur in host cell compartments determined by virus type.
process_flow.mechanism: DNA viruses often replicate in nucleus using host polymerases; RNA viruses replicate in cytoplasm with viral polymerases. Capsid proteins are synthesized on host ribosomes: cytoplasmic for most viruses; ER-bound for envelope glycoproteins. Genome and capsid proteins accumulate at assembly sites: cytoplasm (picornaviruses), nucleus (adenoviruses), ER-Golgi intermediate compartment (coronaviruses), plasma membrane (influenza, HIV).
process_flow.result: Genome copies and capsid proteins concentrated at assembly site; ready for particle formation
applications: Target for antiviral strategies blocking replication or protein synthesis

image.alt: Subcellular sites of viral genome replication and protein synthesis
image.caption: Assembly preparation: DNA viruses (nucleus), RNA viruses (cytoplasm). Capsid proteins synthesized on host ribosomes; glycoproteins on ER-bound ribosomes. Genome and proteins traffic to assembly site.

process_flow.process: Nucleocapsid Formation: Genome Packaging and Capsid Assembly
process_flow.description: Genome is encapsidated by capsid proteins through self-assembly or active packaging mechanisms.
process_flow.mechanism: Two main strategies: (1) Co-assembly: Capsid proteins bind genome during assembly (picornaviruses, TMV); electrostatic interactions between positive capsid interiors and negative nucleic acids drive condensation. (2) Procapsid packaging: Empty capsid assembles with scaffolding proteins → genome pumped in by ATP-driven motor (bacteriophages, herpesviruses) → scaffolding discarded → capsid matures. Packaging signals in genome ensure specific incorporation of viral RNA/DNA over host nucleic acids.
process_flow.result: Nucleocapsid formed: genome protected within protein shell; ready for envelopment or release
applications: Packaging specificity ensures infectious particle formation
Capsid stability balances protection with controlled uncoating
Target for antivirals blocking assembly or packaging

image.alt: Nucleocapsid assembly via co-assembly or procapsid packaging mechanisms
image.caption: Nucleocapsid formation: Co-assembly (genome + capsid proteins condense together) or procapsid packaging (empty capsid + motor-driven genome insertion). Packaging signals ensure viral genome specificity.

process_flow.process: Envelopment and Release: Membrane Acquisition and Budding
process_flow.description: Enveloped viruses acquire lipid bilayer by budding through host membranes, incorporating viral glycoproteins.
process_flow.mechanism: Nucleocapsid traffics to budding site (plasma membrane, ER, Golgi) via interactions between capsid/matrix proteins and glycoprotein cytoplasmic tails. Host ESCRT machinery (or viral homologs) mediates membrane scission, pinching off virion. Budding incorporates glycoproteins while excluding most host proteins. Non-enveloped viruses exit via cell lysis or non-lytic exocytosis. Maturation proteolysis may occur during/after release to activate infectivity.
process_flow.result: Mature virion released: genome protected, surface proteins functional, ready for new infection cycle
applications: Budding site determines envelope composition and glycoprotein processing
ESCRT dependence links viral release to host cell biology
Target for antivirals blocking budding or maturation

image.alt: Viral budding through host membrane showing glycoprotein incorporation and ESCRT-mediated scission
image.caption: Envelopment: Nucleocapsid + glycoproteins at budding site → ESCRT-mediated membrane scission → released virion with host-derived envelope + viral glycoproteins. Maturation proteolysis may activate infectivity post-release.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Structural Virology Techniques and Applications
sections.definition: Modern structural biology techniques—cryo-electron microscopy, X-ray crystallography, and computational modeling—reveal atomic-level details of viral architecture, enabling rational design of antivirals and vaccines.

process_types.type: Cryo-Electron Microscopy: Visualizing Viruses at Near-Atomic Resolution
process_types.energy_profile: Rapid freezing preserves native structure; electron beams image particles without crystallization; computational reconstruction generates 3D maps
process_types.direction: From low-resolution EM images to atomic models revealing protein folds, glycan positions, and conformational states
process_types.function: Determine structures of viral particles, entry complexes, and antibody-bound states to inform therapeutic design
goals: Resolve capsid protein folds and assembly interfaces
Map glycoprotein conformations in prefusion/postfusion states
Characterize drug-binding sites for antiviral development
process_types.energy_source: Electron beams (200-300 keV) interact with frozen-hydrated samples; detectors capture scattered electrons; GPUs accelerate image processing

primary_example.process: SARS-CoV-2 Spike Structure Determination by Cryo-EM
primary_example.explanation: Cryo-EM revealed SARS-CoV-2 spike (S) protein structure at 3.5 Å resolution within weeks of virus identification. Key findings: (1) Prefusion trimer stabilized by proline substitutions (2P mutation) enabled structural studies; (2) Receptor-binding domain (RBD) adopts 'up' (ACE2-accessible) or 'down' (hidden) conformations; (3) N-linked glycans mapped, revealing glycan shield protecting epitopes; (4) Furin cleavage site structure informed vaccine design. These insights guided mRNA vaccine development (stabilizing prefusion S) and monoclonal antibody therapeutics (targeting RBD). Cryo-EM's speed and ability to capture multiple conformations made it ideal for rapid pandemic response.

image.alt: Cryo-EM density map and atomic model of SARS-CoV-2 spike protein
image.caption: Cryo-EM of SARS-CoV-2 spike: Prefusion trimer, RBD 'up/down' conformations, glycan shield mapped. Structure informed vaccine design (prefusion stabilization) and antibody therapeutics (RBD targeting).

image.alt: Cryo-EM workflow from sample preparation to atomic model
image.caption: Cryo-EM workflow: Vitrification (rapid freezing) → electron imaging → particle picking → 2D classification → 3D reconstruction → atomic model building. Enables near-atomic structures without crystallization.

process_types.type: Structure-Based Vaccine Design: From Atomic Models to Immunogens
process_types.energy_profile: Computational design and structural validation enable engineering of stable immunogens that elicit potent neutralizing antibodies
process_types.direction: From native viral structures to engineered immunogens with enhanced stability, immunogenicity, and safety
process_types.function: Design vaccines that present conserved, neutralizing epitopes while avoiding non-protective or variable regions
goals: Stabilize prefusion conformations of fusion proteins
Focus immune response on conserved neutralizing epitopes
Remove glycan shields or variable loops that distract immunity
Design nanoparticle displays to enhance B cell activation
process_types.energy_source: Computational protein design algorithms (Rosetta), structural validation (cryo-EM, X-ray), and immunogenicity testing in animal models

primary_example.process: RSV F Protein Stabilization for Vaccine Development
primary_example.explanation: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) fusion (F) protein undergoes dramatic conformational change from prefusion to postfusion state during entry. Antibodies against prefusion F are 10-100× more potent than postfusion F antibodies. Structural studies identified flexible regions that trigger conformational change. Engineering: Disulfide bonds and cavity-filling mutations (DS-Cav1 design) stabilized prefusion F. Result: Prefusion F immunogens elicit 10× higher neutralizing titers than postfusion F in animals and humans. This structure-based approach enabled first RSV vaccines (Arexvy, Abrysvo) approved in 2023, demonstrating how atomic-level insights translate to clinical impact.

image.alt: RSV F protein prefusion vs. postfusion conformations and stabilizing mutations
image.caption: RSV F stabilization: Prefusion state (neutralizing epitopes exposed) vs. postfusion (epitopes hidden). DS-Cav1 mutations (disulfide, cavity fill) lock prefusion conformation, enhancing vaccine immunogenicity 10-fold.

image.alt: Workflow from viral structure to engineered immunogen for vaccine development
image.caption: Structure-based design: Native structure → epitope mapping → computational stabilization → immunogen production → structural validation → immunogenicity testing. Enables rational vaccine engineering targeting conserved neutralizing sites.

sections.relationship: Structural virology techniques and structure-based design form a feedback loop: high-resolution structures reveal targets for intervention; engineered immunogens and antivirals validate structural hypotheses; iterative refinement improves therapeutic efficacy. This synergy accelerates responses to emerging pathogens.

sections.section_title: Molecular Mechanisms: Structural Determinants of Host Range and Tropism
sections.purpose: Viral surface proteins determine which cells and species a virus can infect by mediating specific interactions with host receptors and entry cofactors.

activation.definition: Host range is dictated by compatibility between viral attachment proteins and host cell surface molecules, with tropism further refined by intracellular factors required for replication.
activation.effect: Receptor binding specificity (e.g., influenza HA for sialic acid linkages) determines which tissues and species are susceptible. Post-entry factors (transcription factors, restriction factors) further limit replication to permissive cells. Structural plasticity in viral proteins enables adaptation to new receptors, driving cross-species transmission and emergence of novel pathogens.

image.alt: Viral surface proteins interacting with host receptors and entry cofactors
image.caption: Host range determinants: Viral attachment proteins (HA, S, gp120) bind specific host receptors (sialic acid, ACE2, CD4). Receptor distribution across tissues/species defines tropism. Structural adaptation enables host switching.

key_properties.property: Receptor Binding Specificity and Cross-Species Transmission
key_properties.explanation: Viral attachment proteins recognize specific host molecules: influenza HA binds sialic acid with specificity for α2,3 (avian) vs. α2,6 (human) linkages; SARS-CoV-2 S protein binds ACE2 with affinity modulated by RBD conformation; HIV gp120 binds CD4 then CCR5/CXCR4 co-receptors. Cross-species transmission requires mutations that alter receptor specificity: avian influenza adapting to human receptors (Q226L, G228S in HA); SARS-related coronaviruses acquiring ACE2 affinity via RBD mutations (N501Y enhances human ACE2 binding). Structural studies identify key residues controlling specificity, enabling surveillance for adaptive mutations with pandemic potential. However, receptor binding is necessary but not sufficient for transmission; intracellular compatibility (polymerase function, immune evasion) also constrains host range.

image.alt: Structural basis of receptor binding specificity for influenza HA and SARS-CoV-2 S protein
image.caption: Receptor specificity: Influenza HA (α2,3 vs. α2,6 sialic acid); SARS-CoV-2 S (ACE2 affinity modulated by RBD residues). Mutations at binding interface enable host adaptation; structural surveillance identifies high-risk variants.

key_properties.property: Conformational Dynamics in Viral Entry Proteins
key_properties.explanation: Viral fusion proteins undergo large conformational changes to merge viral and host membranes: class I (influenza HA, HIV gp41, SARS-CoV-2 S2) refold from prefusion trimers to postfusion hairpins; class II (flavivirus E) rearrange from dimeric to trimeric fusion states. These transitions are triggered by specific cues: low pH (influenza, flaviviruses), receptor binding (HIV), or proteolytic cleavage (coronaviruses). Conformational flexibility enables efficient entry but creates vulnerabilities: prefusion states expose conserved epitopes targeted by broadly neutralizing antibodies; fusion intermediates are transient targets for entry inhibitors. Stabilizing prefusion conformations for vaccines (RSV F, SARS-CoV-2 S) or blocking conformational changes with antivirals (enfuvirtide for HIV) exploits structural dynamics for therapeutic benefit.

image.alt: Conformational changes in class I viral fusion proteins during membrane fusion
image.caption: Fusion mechanism: Prefusion trimer (receptor-bound) → trigger (pH/protease) → extended intermediate (fusion peptide inserted) → hairpin refolding (membranes pulled together). Conformational dynamics enable entry but create therapeutic targets.

key_properties.explanation: Many enveloped viruses decorate surface glycoproteins with host-derived N-linked glycans that shield protein epitopes from antibody recognition. HIV gp120 is ~50% glycan by mass; SARS-CoV-2 S protein has ~22 glycosylation sites per protomer. Glycans are 'self' molecules, poorly immunogenic, and physically block antibody access to underlying protein surfaces. However, glycans also constrain viral evolution: removing glycosylation sites may expose epitopes but can impair protein folding or receptor binding. Some broadly neutralizing antibodies penetrate the glycan shield by recognizing glycan-protein composite epitopes (e.g., HIV PGT121 family binds glycan at N332 + protein loop). Understanding glycan shielding informs vaccine design: engineering immunogens that focus responses on conserved, glycan-shielded epitopes may elicit broader protection.

image.alt: Glycan shielding of viral glycoproteins and antibody recognition strategies
image.caption: Glycan shield: Host-derived N-glycans mask protein epitopes from antibodies. Broadly neutralizing antibodies (e.g., HIV PGT121) recognize glycan-protein composite epitopes. Vaccine design aims to focus responses on conserved shielded sites.

summary: Virus structure comprises three fundamental components: nucleic acid genome (DNA or RNA, single- or double-stranded, segmented or non-segmented), protein capsid (arranged in icosahedral, helical, or complex symmetry), and optional lipid envelope derived from host membranes. Genome type influences replication strategy and mutation rate: DNA viruses generally use host polymerases with proofreading (lower mutation rates); RNA viruses use error-prone viral polymerases (high mutation rates enabling rapid adaptation). Capsid architecture follows geometric principles: icosahedral symmetry maximizes volume with minimal genetic information; helical symmetry scales with genome length; complex viruses (bacteriophages) combine multiple symmetries. Enveloped viruses acquire lipid bilayers by budding through host membranes, incorporating viral glycoproteins that mediate receptor binding and membrane fusion; envelopes confer efficient entry but reduce environmental stability. Structural features determine host range (receptor specificity), transmission routes (envelope sensitivity), and vulnerability to disinfectants. Modern structural biology techniques (cryo-EM, X-ray crystallography) reveal atomic-level details enabling rational design of antivirals and vaccines. Understanding viral architecture is essential for predicting emergence of novel pathogens, developing countermeasures, and appreciating the evolutionary optimization of these minimal infectious entities.
